summary:
Napoleon's invasion of Russia in 1812, known as the Russian campaign or the Patriotic War in Russian historical tradition, was one of the greatest military disasters in history and marked the beginning of the end of French imperial dominance in Europe. Napoleon assembled his Grande Armée — the largest European army ever fielded to that point, numbering approximately 685,000 men drawn from France and its client states across the continent — and crossed the Niemen River into Russia in June 1812.

The Russian strategy, whether deliberate or forced by circumstances, was to avoid a decisive pitched battle and retreat into the vast Russian interior, drawing the French deeper into hostile territory and stretching their supply lines to breaking point. The one major engagement, the [[event:2525|Battle of Borodino]] fought in September, was enormously bloody — roughly 70,000 casualties combined — but tactically inconclusive. Napoleon failed to achieve the encirclement and destruction of the Russian army he sought, and when he entered Moscow shortly afterward, he found it had been largely evacuated and deliberately set ablaze.

The French occupation of Moscow, which Napoleon expected to end with Russian peace overtures, instead produced silence from Tsar Alexander I. After five weeks, Napoleon was forced to begin a retreat in mid-October. The withdrawal became a catastrophe. Soldiers who had survived the advance were ravaged by disease, starvation, exposure to an early and brutal winter, and relentless harassment from Cossack cavalry and partisan forces. The crossing of the Beresina River in November, with Russian forces closing in from multiple directions, cost the French another 30,000 men.

Of the original Grande Armée, fewer than 100,000 combat-effective troops returned from Russia. The campaign destroyed the myth of Napoleonic invincibility, emboldened the European powers to form new coalitions, and set in motion the chain of events that led to [[event:2522|Waterloo]] three years later. Russia's successful defense became a founding myth of Russian national identity, commemorated in art, literature, and music — most famously in Tolstoy's War and Peace and Tchaikovsky's 1812 Overture.
